nativeRange.habitat: Adaptable to a variety of freshwater habitats, including murky still waters, slow-flowing rivers, streams, and clearwater channels
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical freshwater rivers and streams, often sheltered and heavily vegetated

decorPreferences: soft fine sand or rounded gravel substrate
rocks and caves for territory and spawning sites
hardy epiphyte plants (Anubias, Java fern, Java moss) — rooted plants may be uprooted due to digging

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality cichlid pellets or granules as a staple. Benefits from regular offerings of frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, chopped mussel, prawn, and earthworms. Occasional vegetable-based flake or blanched greens help round out the diet. Twice-daily feeding is commonly advised.

avoidWith: small fish that may be predated

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by 5snake5; 4876x2580. Tack-sharp full lateral shot of the electric blue morph over a green planted background, showing the vivid iridescent electric-blue body and yellow-edged dorsal fin the color line is prized for.

Photo by Shamekh.yasser; 1740x1725. Front three-quarter view of the electric blue morph under blue/actinic aquarium lighting; sharp on the head and clearly the correct morph, kept as a second angle. Moody lighting so lower ranked.

summary: The Electric Blue Acara is a selectively bred color line of Andinoacara pulcher, prized for its vivid iridescent blue coloration. It tends to be calmer than many cichlids of comparable size and generally breeds reliably in captivity, making it a popular choice for hobbyists moving beyond smaller dwarf cichlids.
careNotes: The Electric Blue Acara is a selectively bred captive line of Andinoacara pulcher; wild-caught individuals of the base species are notably less intensely colored. The genus was formerly classified as Aequidens and was moved to Andinoacara in 2009. Compared to many other cichlids, this species is widely considered tolerant of a broad range of pH and water hardness, which makes it a natural next step for hobbyists who have kept smaller dwarf cichlids such as Rams or Apistogrammas and are ready for a mid-sized species. That said, it remains sensitive to deteriorating water quality, so efficient biological filtration and consistent partial water changes are generally advised. The Electric Blue Acara tends to dig, so rooted plants are best replaced with epiphytes (plants such as Anubias or Java fern that grow attached to rock or wood rather than in the substrate) secured to hardscape. Territorial behavior is most pronounced during spawning, and a spacious tank with clearly defined zones or line-of-sight breaks — driftwood, rocks, or dense planting — can help reduce aggression toward tankmates. Pairs tend to bond strongly and will cooperatively defend their eggs and fry. Although the Electric Blue Acara is sometimes described in the hobby as a hybrid, it is broadly regarded as a selectively bred line of A. pulcher rather than a cross between two distinct species.
breedingNotes: Pair-bonding substrate-spawners; pairs have been reported to mate for life and will defend spawning sites vigorously. The female typically lays 150–200 adhesive eggs on a flat, cleaned surface (rock, broad leaf, or substrate depression). Eggs generally hatch within 48–72 hours at typical aquarium temperatures, and fry are reported to become free-swimming around 4–5 days after hatching. Both parents guard and fan the eggs and continue to herd fry for approximately 2 weeks. Sexual maturity is commonly reached at 8–10 months or around 10 cm body length. Breeding is generally considered easy for a cichlid of this size.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary source after Aquarium Co-Op blog returned 404. SeriouslyFish is the first available secondary per slice source-urls.json. Provided scientific name, taxonomy, native range, water parameters, tank dimensions, diet, temperament, and breeding data.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-verification secondary. Confirmed scientific name, family, pH 6.0–7.5 (slightly narrower than SeriouslyFish 6.5–8.0; averaged to 6.0–8.0), temperature 20–27°C (averaged with SeriouslyFish 22–28°C to yield 21–28°C), hardness 6–20 dGH. Tank size listed as 30 gal (114L) for a single fish; conservative pair minimum of 200L adopted per playbook bias-toward-larger rule and community consensus from Aquarium Co-Op forum. Lifespan 10–20 years from this source only.

fish.breedingNotes: The Electric Blue Acara is a reliable pair-bonding, biparental substrate-spawner. The female typically lays 150–200 adhesive eggs on a flat, cleaned surface, which generally hatch within 48–72 hours at typical aquarium temperatures. Fry are reported to become free-swimming around 4–5 days after hatching, with both parents actively guarding and herding the young for approximately two weeks.
